I only got a thousand shares but still. But anyway, so before he disappeared to wherever he is, Stern had about the same numbers. What was Stern doing? He was really selling permission. So he was making it okay for adult males to behave like 14 year olds, right? And that this was acceptable behavior, right? So he was selling permission. You would get a big permission slip is what you were getting. So the great positioning for you is either as their champion or as their expert or as their savior, the guy riding to the rescue. There's probably three or four if we think about it. You want to pick the one that you feel most comfortable with and then you want to embellish the hell out of it. You know, you want to make yourself bigger than life in that category. But do you have the basis for something that makes sense? Yes. But you want to transcend how it would ordinarily be handled, which is just typical classic value added selling. Does that make sense to you? Okay. Are you guys done? Does somebody else want to come up and play? Oh, you didn't play yet, did you? No. Go. And there's another chair up here. And one of these guys is going to leave or get thrown out of here or something. So come on up. You're going to speak to joint ventures tomorrow? Yeah. The omnipresent you said about finding the public you want, whatever they are, finding that target, and the omnipresence, are there obviously going to be things you would do and would not do, depending upon the public themselves, correct? Yes and no. So let's think about, let's think about something simple. Let's think about apparel. Apparel? Close. Okay. So one way to have somebody think about you is they put on a piece of clothing that has your logo, your slogan, your something on it, right? So a bar is going to do that with t-shirts. Motorcycle shops are going to do it probably with t-shirts. Tattoo parlor, I don't know. I guess they're going to do it with sleeveless. We got t-shirts, but we also got nice looking polo shirts. We got dress shirts. But you ought to understand we don't have those just because Bill was in the clothing business and can't resist the impulse to continue to be in the clothing business. That may be a reason, but it's not the primary reason. And actually I doubt that it's a reason. We don't have them just because people buy them and there's a profit in them because it's such a small part of the business. It's probably not even worth the hassle and the inventory on its surface, right? We have it because of cultural indoctrination. Because if I can't, first of all, if I got a bunch of people walking around in an event wearing our clothing that's helpful. More importantly, if I got them wearing it at home, that's helpful. So now we sell $500,000 financial plans to CEOs. It ain't go be t-shirts, but I'd like them wearing something. Now, if it's not a peril, maybe it's really nice watches. Maybe it's cuff lengths. So now think about doohickeys that you populate an environment with. Paper wades, desks, ornaments. Crap. Glassware. On and on and on. Where you're omnipresent in their environment. Think media. So should it be a glossy magazine? Should it be an oversized magazine? Should it be a newsletter? Should it be garish neon postcards with cute cartoons on them? But it damn sure ought to be something. Because basically by every means and media there is I want to be in the customer's life on a day to day basis and I want to be in the place in the market that I've identified as mine as often, as frequently, as constantly as I possibly can. Now we're not Disney, but again, as case study, I mean, they're everywhere.
